one_line_takeaway: A tampered pretrained model can act as a latching 'data trap' that survives full finetuning and lets an attacker reconstruct individual private finetuning samples (white-box) or run perfect membership inference (black-box), proving DP-SGD's worst-case bound is near-tight end-to-end and that loose DP budgets are unsafe under an untrusted-model supply chain.
key_finding: Weight-tampering of a shared pretrained model can guarantee reconstruction of individual finetuning samples, not merely membership. White-box reconstructions of full images (ViT on Caltech 101) and full sentences (BERT on TREC-50) are recognizable/near-perfect while the backdoored models retain usable downstream accuracy (ViT 83% on Caltech 101, BERT 78% on TREC-50). Black-box perfect membership inference exposes that DP-SGD's 'pessimistic' worst-case analysis is nearly tight even for a realistic end-to-end attacker who only sees the final model: the empirically observed privacy leakage closely matches the theoretical upper bound. Consequently, the loose privacy budgets used in practice (e.g., epsilon >= 8-9 in Ramaswamy et al. 2020's production federated system) are insecure when the pretrained model is untrusted. Black-box data reconstruction of a captured CIFAR-10 image from a backdoored MLP takes under 10,000 queries.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'privacy backdoors': a malicious pretrained model whose weights have been tampered with so that, after a victim downloads and finetunes it, individual finetuning examples can be reconstructed. The central technical novelty is a 'single-use data trap' backdoor. Unlike prior gradient-inversion attacks in Federated Learning (Fowl et al. 2021), where the attacker observes a single gradient update, here the trap must survive an entire full-finetuning run (multiple epochs, thousands of SGD steps). The authors achieve this with a 'latch'-like construction: once a backdoor neuron fires on a training input and writes that input into the weights, the resulting gradient shuts the neuron down permanently (proven to happen with probability 1-1/C, where C is the number of classes, because a fired input is misclassified and yields a large positive shutdown gradient). They build this for MLPs and, as the main engineering contribution, for real pretrained transformers (ViT-B/32 and BERT-base) via a multi-module design (input/backdoor/amplifier/erasure/signal-propagation/output modules) plus numerical tricks to tame layer-normalization coupling and GELU activations. Beyond white-box weight-reading, the paper shows (a) perfect (100%) black-box membership inference, used to build the first tight end-to-end attack on DP-SGD, and (b) black-box data reconstruction by reducing weight recovery to model-stealing critical-point queries.
threat_model: A malicious model provider (supply-chain attacker, e.g., someone uploading to HuggingFace) tampers with a pretrained model's weights ONCE, before the victim downloads it. The victim then performs full finetuning (adds a new linear classification head and updates the entire model with SGD over multiple epochs) on their own private classification dataset. The attacker never observes intermediate gradients or training steps (weaker than the Federated Learning threat model). Two access levels to the resulting finetuned model are considered: white-box (attacker reads the finetuned weights, enabling the strongest reconstruction of captured inputs) and black-box (query-only access, enabling perfect membership inference and, for simple models, data reconstruction). The attacker needs only a loose approximation of the input distribution to calibrate backdoor biases.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) Only full finetuning is handled; parameter-efficient methods like LoRA are left to future work, which is a major coverage gap given LoRA's popularity. (2) The shutdown guarantee is probabilistic (1-1/C per backdoor); to keep the model from breaking down, experiments deliberately use FEWER backdoors than output classes, so per-run capture counts are modest ('dozens' of examples). (3) Reported transformer results are from finetuning runs where benign utility did NOT collapse; failing backdoors (correctly-classified inputs that re-open and produce runaway gradients) can destroy utility, and the authors acknowledge a victim would notice a failed run and retry, i.e., results are conditioned on successful runs. (4) The backdoor construction removes a fraction of the pretrained model's feature representations and encoder blocks and reduces test accuracy versus a clean model. (5) ViT reconstructions are grayscaled/downscaled for space efficiency and show brightness artifacts from layer normalization. (6) Black-box full data reconstruction is demonstrated only for a simple MLP, not transformers. (7) The attacker must know a loose approximation of the input distribution to set biases. Not independently flagged but notable: no defense/detection method is proposed beyond noting that using ReLU + benign-only layernorm would make the backdoor 'too obvious'.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (MLP warmup and black-box MLP reconstruction)
quantitative_results: Backdoored ViT-B/32 reaches 83% test accuracy on Caltech 101 while still leaking reconstructable images
Backdoored BERT-base reaches 78% test accuracy on TREC-50 with near-perfect sentence reconstructions
Black-box membership inference is perfect (100% accuracy) at determining training-set presence
Black-box data reconstruction of a captured CIFAR-10 input from a backdoored MLP requires under 10,000 queries (2 queries per input coordinate via ReLU critical-point finding)
Each backdoor shuts down (and thus succeeds) with probability 1-1/C; expected captures from k calibrated backdoors is k*(1-1/C), ~99.9% per-backdoor for ImageNet-scale C
Empirical DP privacy leakage for an end-to-end attacker nearly matches the provable DP-SGD upper bound (Figure 5), across theoretical epsilon values ~1-8
MLP backdoor setup: 3-layer perceptron, 256 hidden units of which 64 are backdoors, CIFAR-10 inputs flattened/scaled to [0,1]^3072
ViT and BERT internal feature dimension d=768 partitioned into [ft, key, act]
Loose production DP budgets (epsilon >= 8-9, Ramaswamy et al. 2020) shown to be insecure under an untrusted model provider
baselines_compared: Fowl et al. 2021 ('Robbing the Fed') Federated Learning gradient-inversion attack — extended here to survive multi-step finetuning rather than a single observed gradient step

Concurrent privacy-backdoor work Wen et al. 2024 and Liu et al. 2024 (Precurious) — noted as weaker attacker goals (membership / no-guarantee extraction) but weaker (black-box-only) assumptions; described as incomparable
DP-SGD theoretical privacy upper bound (Abadi et al. 2016) — the attack's empirical lower bound is compared against it to demonstrate tightness
Nasr et al. 2021 adversary-instantiation lower bounds — tight only for all-steps attackers; this work targets the end-to-end (final-model-only) attacker
Exact model-stealing attacks (Tramer et al. 2016; Carlini et al. 2020) — reused as the mechanism for black-box weight recovery
